items.question: In the bias–variance decomposition, bias measures:
options: Sensitivity of predictions to which training sample is used
Systematic error from E[ŷ] deviating from f*(x)
Label noise that no model can remove
Demographic disparity across user groups
items.answer: Systematic error from E[ŷ] deviating from f*(x)
items.explanation: Bias² = (E[ŷ] − f*(x))² captures average miss from model assumptions; variance captures sample sensitivity; Var(ε) is irreducible noise.

items.question: Which symptom best indicates high bias (underfitting)?
options: Low train error, high validation error
High train error and high validation error
Perfect train error, random validation error
Low train and low validation error
items.answer: High train error and high validation error
items.explanation: Underfitting from high bias hurts performance on both splits because the model is too simple to capture the pattern.

items.question: Can adding unlimited training data eliminate high bias from a misspecified model?
options: Yes — more data always reaches zero error
No — bias reflects structural limits of the hypothesis class
Yes, but only for neural networks
No, because bias equals variance at large n
items.answer: No — bias reflects structural limits of the hypothesis class
items.explanation: Extra data shrinks variance; if the model cannot represent f*, squared bias remains.

items.question: Fitting a straight line to clearly curved data primarily increases:
options: Variance only
Bias (systematic miss of curvature)
Irreducible noise
Label leakage
items.explanation: A line systematically under/over-predicts at the ends — E[ŷ] misses f*(x), classic high bias.

items.question: Which change most directly reduces bias?
options: Collect 10× more training rows without changing the model
Increase model capacity or add expressive features
Stronger L2 regularization
Smaller validation set
items.answer: Increase model capacity or add expressive features
items.explanation: Richer hypotheses lower systematic error; more data mainly helps variance; stronger regularization typically increases bias.

items.question: Bias² appears in expected squared error as:
options: E[(ŷ − E[ŷ])²]
Var(ε) only
Train loss minus validation loss
items.explanation: Standard decomposition: bias² + variance of ŷ + irreducible label noise.
